sections.content:

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.

Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so after clicking on the blue shape:
Current:
- Checkerboard: [20:23, 18:21] (4x4) - top-right
- Gray: [30:33, 8:11] (4x4) - middle-left
- Red: [30:33, 12:17] (4x6) - middle-center
- Dark red: [30:33, 18:21] (4x4) - middle-right
- Blue rect: [34:37, 8:11] (4x4) - bottom-left
- Light green: [42:43, 8:9] (bottom-left)
Initial:
- Blue rect: [20:23, 18:21] (4x4) - top-right
- Gray: [30:33, 8:11] (4x4) - middle-left
- Red: [30:33, 12:17] (4x6) - middle-center
- Dark red: [30:33, 18:21] (4x4) - middle-right
- Checkerboard: [34:37, 8:11] (4x4) - bottom-left
- Light green: [40:41, 10:11] (center)
So the blue rect and checkerboard swapped positions. The light green square is still at bottom-left [42:43, 8:9].
Let me think about what the goal might be. I've been clicking on the right shapes, which rearranges the left blocks. But the level hasn't completed yet. Maybe the goal is not just about rearranging the blocks, but also about positioning the colored squares.
Let me think about the colored squares:
- Yellow square: in the top gray box, at center [20:21, 24:25]
- Light green square: in the bottom gray box, at bottom-left [42:43, 8:9]
The right shapes have a "stepped" pattern:
```
  . X .
  . X .
  X X X
```
The yellow square is at center (middle-center), which is one of the "X" cells. The light green square is at bottom-left, which is also one of the "X" cells.
But the level hasn't completed. Maybe I need to move the light green square to a different position using the arrow keys.
Let me try moving the light green square to different positions and see if the level completes. The bottom gray box is a 3x3 grid of 2x2 cells. The light green square is currently at bottom-left [42:43, 8:9].
Let me try moving it to bottom-center [42:43, 10:11] using RIGHT.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try moving the light green square to different positions
# Currently at bottom-left [42:43, 8:9]
# Let me try moving it to bottom-center using RIGHT

predict(expect_change=True, note='Move light green square RIGHT to bottom-center')
action(['RIGHT'])

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== Left blocks after RIGHT ===")
for r in range(38, 44):
    line = ascii_lines[r][7:22]
    if any(c not in 'c' for c in line):
        print(f"  {r}: {line}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"\n  Light green: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

print(f"\n  Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  43: cggNNggcccccccc
